svg.text: SOAR-Light C2C: Current Event-Driven vs Proposed Scheduled Flow
How removing the grpc-in pod saves 50% of Kubernetes resources for scheduled flows
1 Customer creates flow via CDC API
CreateFlow() → creates BOTH InSvc + OutSvc resources
3 Kubernetes Resources Created (2 pods, 2 ConfigMaps, 2 StatefulSets)
BloxOne event → Kafka topic → grpc-in reads event → gRPC call → soar-light receives
→ processEvent() → runDataFlow() → detects DataTypes=["DNS"] → IEventHandler path
→ User Python script processes event data → sends to downstream API/SIEM
Expects 2 ConfigMaps, 2 StatefulSets per flow. Reconciles both InSvc and OutSvc.
Total per flow: 2 Pods | 2 ConfigMaps | 2 StatefulSets
1 Customer creates flow via CDC API
CreateFlow() → InSvc==nil? skip. Only OutSvc created.
Key insight: grpc-in pod is completely skipped — no ConfigMap, no StatefulSet
3 Kubernetes Resources Created (1 pod, 1 ConfigMap, 1 StatefulSet)
Pod starts → reads FLOW_JSON → svc.GetCron().Start() (Change 7: starts ticker)
→ ProcessScheduledScript() → creates Python venv → robfig/cron registers job
→ Every 5 min: cron ticker → infoblox_driver.py --script-schedule → IScheduler.schedule(ctx)
Expects 1 ConfigMap (not 2). Skips InSvc sync. Only reconciles OutSvc (soar-light).
Total per flow: 1 Pod | 1 ConfigMap | 1 StatefulSet
cdc.flow.api.service (6 changes): Unblock validation → nil InSvc → nil-safe CRUD → fix sync loop → fix CM count
soar-light (1 change): Add cron.Start() in cmd/cloud/main.go so robfig/cron ticker fires for scheduled jobs
No changes needed: Python driver, CDC API contract, on-prem flows, existing event-driven C2C flows
